pr.title: Secrets System Phase 5 and 6 Implementation: Proxy-First Execution and Runtime Hardening

comments.body: You have reached your Codex usage limits for code reviews. You can see your limits in the [Codex usage dashboard](https://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/usage).

## Unused local variable Variable existing is not used. --- In general, to fix an unused local variable, either remove the variable (and often the corresponding computation) if it is genuinely unnecessary, or keep the operation but stop assigning it to a variable if only its side effects are needed. This eliminates dead code and clarifies intent without changing behavior.

Here, the most conservative change is to keep the await SecretsService.get_secret(...) call (in case it has side effects) but remove the assignment to existing, since the result is never used. Concretely, in api_service/api/routers/secrets.py inside create_secret, replace the line existing = await SecretsService.get_secret(db, request.slug) with just await SecretsService.get_secret(db, request.slug). No additional imports or definitions are required, and the rest of the function remains unchanged.

## Unused import Import of 'Type' is not used. --- In general, to fix an unused import, remove the specific name that is not used from the import statement, keeping only the names that are actually referenced, or delete the entire import if nothing from it is used. This cleans up the code, eliminates linter warnings, and has no effect on runtime semantics.

Here, the best fix is to adjust the import on line 2 in moonmind/auth/resolvers/base.py so that it imports only Mapping from typing. Mapping is used in the MasterSecretResolver.__init__ signature, while Type is never referenced. Concretely, change from typing import Mapping, Type to from typing import Mapping. No other code changes, methods, or definitions are required.

## Unused import Import of 'InvalidToken' is not used. --- To fix an unused import, you remove the import statement (or the unused name from a multi-name import) so that only actually-used symbols are imported. This reduces clutter and avoids confusing future maintainers about intended behavior.

Here, the best targeted fix is to delete the single unused import line from cryptography.fernet import InvalidToken in moonmind/auth/resolvers/db_resolver.py. No other code in the snippet depends on this name, and we will not alter the exception handling behavior; we only remove the unused import. This means editing line 2 of moonmind/auth/resolvers/db_resolver.py to remove it entirely, leaving the rest of the file intact.

No new methods, imports, or definitions are required to implement this change.

## Unused import Import of 'update' is not used. --- To fix the problem, remove the unused update symbol from the SQLAlchemy import so that only actually used names are imported. This eliminates dead code, keeps imports clean, and satisfies the static analysis rule without affecting runtime behavior.

Concretely, in api_service/services/secrets.py, on the line from sqlalchemy import select, update, the code only uses select(...) throughout the class methods. update is never referenced. The best fix is to edit that import line to only import select:

  • Change from sqlalchemy import select, update
  • To from sqlalchemy import select

No other methods, imports, or definitions are needed since select is already used and update is not used anywhere.

## Unused import Import of 'Path' is not used. --- In general, an unused import should be removed to keep the code clean and avoid confusion about dependencies. Since no symbol named Path is used in this test file, and tmp_path (pytest fixture) already provides a pathlib.Path-like object where needed, we can safely delete the import line.

Concretely, in tests/unit/core/test_encryption.py, remove the line from pathlib import Path (line 3). No other lines need to be updated, and no new imports or definitions are required. This change does not alter any existing functionality, as the imported name is never referenced.

## Unused import Import of 'SecuritySettings' is not used. --- To fix an unused import, the general approach is simply to remove the import statement (or the unused name from a multi-name import) so that the code no longer carries dead, unnecessary dependencies.

Here, the single best fix is to delete the line from moonmind.config.settings import SecuritySettings from tests/unit/core/test_encryption.py. No other changes are needed: all tests operate using mocks and do not reference SecuritySettings, and removing this line will not alter test logic or behavior. The exact region to change is line 10 of tests/unit/core/test_encryption.py, leaving the surrounding imports intact.

No new methods, imports, or definitions are required to implement this change.

## Unused import Import of 'os' is not used. --- To fix an unused import, remove it or start actually using it. Since the existing tests do not require os and already use pytest’s monkeypatch fixture for environment manipulation, the best fix is to delete the unused import os line.

Concretely, in tests/unit/auth/test_secret_resolvers.py, remove line 1 (import os) and leave the rest of the imports unchanged. No new methods, imports, or definitions are needed; this is a straightforward cleanup that does not alter any test behavior.

## Unused import Import of 'MagicMock' is not used. --- To fix an unused import, the general approach is to remove the specific name from the import statement (or the whole import line if none of its names are used). This keeps the code cleaner and avoids confusion about dependencies.

Here, we should edit tests/unit/auth/test_secret_resolvers.py to remove MagicMock from the from unittest.mock import ... line while leaving patch and AsyncMock intact, as they are clearly used throughout the tests. No other changes are required, and no behavior will change because MagicMock was never referenced.

Concretely, in tests/unit/auth/test_secret_resolvers.py at line 3, change:

from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock, AsyncMock

to:

from unittest.mock import patch, AsyncMock

No additional imports, methods, or definitions are needed.

## Unused import Import of 'AdapterVaultSecretResolver' is not used. --- To fix the problem, remove the unused symbol from the import statement so that only actually used resolvers are imported. This avoids altering any runtime behavior while satisfying the static analysis rule.

Concretely, in tests/unit/auth/test_secret_resolvers.py, adjust the multi-line import from moonmind.auth.resolvers to drop AdapterVaultSecretResolver from the list. Keep the order and formatting of the remaining imported names (EnvSecretResolver, DbEncryptedSecretResolver, ExecSecretResolver, MasterSecretResolver) unchanged apart from deleting the unused one. No additional methods, definitions, or imports are needed.

## Unused import Import of 'datetime' is not used. --- To fix an unused import, the general approach is to remove the import statement (or the unused symbol from a multi-symbol import) when it is not used anywhere in the file. This cleans up the code, avoids confusion, and silences static analysis warnings without changing runtime behavior.

In this case, the best fix is to delete the line from datetime import datetime at the top of tests/unit/services/test_secrets.py. The rest of the file does not depend on datetime, so no additional changes are required. Only that single line needs to be removed; all other imports should remain as-is.

![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

There is a Time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerability when creating the local encryption key file. An attacker could create a symbolic link at `local_secret_path` between the `exists()` check and the `write_text()` call, potentially causing the key to be written to an unintended location.

To fix this, you should use `os.open` with `O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_EXCL` flags to atomically create the file with the correct permissions. This ensures the operation fails if the file already exists and avoids the race condition.

Example of a safer implementation:
```python
# Write securely using an atomic open to prevent TOCTOU race conditions.
try:
    fd = os.open(local_secret_path, os.O_WRONLY | os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL, 0o600)
    with os.fdopen(fd, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        f.write(new_key)
except FileExistsError:
    # This can happen in a race. Overwriting is the fallback.
    local_secret_path.write_text(new_key, encoding="utf-8")
    os.chmod(local_secret_path, stat.S_IRUSR | stat.S_IWUSR)
```

![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The error message from `stderr` is logged directly when an `exec` command fails. This could potentially leak sensitive information if the executed command writes secrets or other sensitive data to its standard error stream.

You should use the `SecretRedactor` utility, which is already being introduced in this PR in `moonmind/workflows/temporal/runtime/launcher.py`, to scrub the `stderr` output before logging it. You will need to import it first:
`from moonmind.utils.logging import SecretRedactor`

```python
            redactor = SecretRedactor.from_environ()
            logger.warning("exec_returned_non_zero", command=command, returncode=proc.returncode, error=redactor.scrub(error_msg))
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

This validation logic in `_validate_secret_refs_update` is identical to `_validate_secret_refs` in the `ProviderProfileCreate` class. To improve maintainability and adhere to the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle, this logic should be extracted into a common helper function at the module level that both validators can call.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The logic for resolving provider keys, constructing target URLs, and setting authentication headers is hardcoded for each provider (`anthropic`, `openai`, `minimax`) across this file. This makes adding or modifying providers cumbersome and error-prone.

Consider refactoring this into a centralized configuration structure, like a dictionary mapping provider IDs to their settings (base URL, auth method, secret key names). This would make the code more maintainable, extensible, and easier to read.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The check for an existing secret is inefficient. It fetches all secret metadata from the database (`SecretsService.list_metadata`) and then iterates through them in Python to find a matching slug. This can lead to performance issues as the number of secrets grows.

A more efficient approach would be to perform a targeted database query to check for the existence of just the specific slug. Consider adding a dedicated service method like `SecretsService.slug_exists(db, slug)` that executes a `SELECT 1 FROM ...` or a `count()` query, and use that here instead.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The logic for setting up proxy environment variables (`..._BASE_URL`, `..._API_KEY`) is hardcoded for each provider (`anthropic`, `openai`, `minimax`). This makes it difficult to add or modify providers and duplicates logic from the proxy router.

This logic should be driven by a centralized configuration, ideally sharing the same configuration source as the proxy router (`api_service/api/routers/proxy.py`). This would improve maintainability and reduce code duplication.

## Code Review

This pull request implements a comprehensive Secrets Management System and Proxy-First execution architecture. It introduces a new ManagedSecret database model, a multi-backend resolver system (supporting DB, Env, Exec, and Vault), and a transparent API proxy for LLM providers to prevent raw credential leakage to agent runtimes. Key additions include a dedicated Secrets Manager UI, automated encryption key generation/retrieval, and log redaction for sensitive data. Feedback focuses on addressing a TOCTOU vulnerability in the encryption key initialization, preventing potential secret leaks in execution logs via the redactor, and improving code maintainability by deduplicating validation logic and centralizing provider-specific proxy configurations. Additionally, a performance optimization was suggested for the secret existence check in the API.
`datetime` is imported but unused in this test module. If the repo runs Ruff/flake8 against tests, this will fail unused-import checks; remove it.
`uuid4` is imported but unused in this test module. If tests are linted, this will fail unused-import checks; remove it.
`os` is imported but unused in this test module. If tests are linted, remove the unused import to avoid failing unused-import checks.

`is_proxy_first` currently disables the base-env filtering for *all* `runtime_env_overrides` keys via `if not _should_filter_base_env_var(ks) or is_proxy_first:`. That means a profile tagged `proxy-first` can still inject sensitive env vars (e.g. `*_API_KEY`, `*_TOKEN`) through `runtime_env_overrides`, defeating the goal of proxy-first execution. Restrict this exception to a small allowlist of the proxy-specific keys you intentionally inject (e.g. `MOONMIND_PROXY_TOKEN`, `*_BASE_URL`, and possibly `*_API_KEY` only when the value is the synthetic proxy token), and keep filtering for everything else.

This file refers to DB secret refs as `db_encrypted:...`, but the new `parse_secret_ref` validator enforces `<backend>://<locator>` (e.g. `db://my-slug`). Keeping `db_encrypted:` here will cause provider-profile validation (and proxy resolution) to diverge from tests/documentation. Update the test profile to use the canonical `db://...` (or `env://...`) format.

`create_secret` checks for conflicts by loading *all* secret metadata (`list_metadata`) and scanning in Python. This is inefficient as the number of secrets grows and also does extra work on every create. Prefer a targeted `SELECT ... WHERE slug = :slug` (or attempt insert and catch `IntegrityError` on the unique index) to detect conflicts.
```suggestion
    # The get_secret only returns ACTIVE ones. If an ACTIVE secret with this slug
    # already exists, we should not create another one.
    if existing is not None:
```

`_resolve_provider_key` looks for secret refs starting with `db_encrypted:` and then passes the ref into `resolver.resolve_secret(...)`. The new `secret_refs` validator requires `<backend>://<locator>` (e.g. `db://anthropic-api-key`), so this fallback will never match and resolution will break for proxy-first profiles. Normalize/parse refs using `parse_secret_ref`, and only allow the backends you intend to support in the proxy (likely `db://` only).

`EnvSecretResolver.resolve` returns an empty string when the env var exists but is blank (it only logs a warning). For credentials, treating empty as “resolved” will typically cause confusing downstream failures. Consider raising `SecretMissingError` when the env var is empty/whitespace so callers can handle it as a missing secret consistently.
```suggestion
            raise SecretMissingError(
                f"Environment variable is empty or whitespace-only: {locator}"
            )
```

This file uses `Record<string, any>` for `details` (and `err: any` in mutation handlers). The repo’s ESLint config enforces `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` as an error, so this will fail CI. Use `unknown`/`Record<string, unknown>` (and narrow as needed) instead of `any`.

This component uses explicit `any` types (`details: Record<string, any>` and `onError: (err: any) => ...`). With `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` set to `error`, this will fail linting. Replace with `unknown` (or a typed error shape) and narrow/cast where appropriate.

There are no unit tests in this PR covering the new proxy router behavior (token decode failures, provider mismatch, header rewriting, and successful pass-through). Given this is a security-critical boundary, add focused unit tests for `api_service/api/routers/proxy.py` to validate request/response handling and secret resolution logic.

`existing = await SecretsService.get_secret(...)` is assigned but never used. This will fail Ruff’s unused-variable check (F841) if enabled for this module; remove it or incorporate it into the conflict check logic.
```suggestion
    # Check if a secret with this slug already exists before creating a new one.
    existing = await SecretsService.get_secret(db, request.slug)
    if existing:
```

`update` is imported from SQLAlchemy but unused in this module. Remove it to avoid unused-import lint failures.
```suggestion
from sqlalchemy import select
```

`provider = profile.get("provider_id", "anthropic").strip().lower()` will raise if `provider_id` is present but `None` (or not a string). Consider normalizing with `str(profile.get("provider_id") or "anthropic")` (and optionally validating allowed providers) before calling `.strip().lower()`.
```suggestion
            provider = str(profile.get("provider_id") or "anthropic").strip().lower()
```

Proxy tokens are minted here using the same master encryption key used for DB secret encryption. This creates two concrete problems: (1) the Temporal worker must have access to `ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY` (or a generated fallback), which expands the trust boundary; and (2) if `ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY` is unset and both the worker and `api_service` auto-generate keys independently, the proxy token cannot be decrypted by the proxy endpoint. Prefer minting a signed/expiring token with an API-service-only secret (or have `api_service` mint tokens), and require a single stable key source shared between components.

`proxy.py` imports `MasterSecretResolver` from `moonmind.auth.secrets`, but there is no such module/class in the codebase (the new resolver lives under `moonmind.auth.resolvers`). As written, this router will fail to import at runtime. Update the import and resolution flow to use the new `parse_secret_ref` + `MasterSecretResolver` API (or call `SecretsService.get_secret` directly for `db://...` refs).

This implementation auto-generates and persists a new encryption key when `ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY` is unset. In multi-process/multi-container deployments this can lead to irrecoverable secrets (key changes on restart) and also breaks proxy-token decryption if different components generate different local keys. Consider requiring an explicit key source in non-dev environments (or a shared Docker secret/volume) and failing fast if a stable key cannot be resolved.
```suggestion
    #    This is only allowed when explicitly enabled via env to avoid
    #    accidental key divergence in multi-process/container deployments.
    allow_auto_generation = os.getenv("MOONMIND_ALLOW_LOCAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY_GENERATION", "").lower() in {
        "1",
        "true",
        "yes",
        "on",
        "dev",
    }

    if not allow_auto_generation:
        logger.error(
            "encryption_key_unconfigured_and_auto_generation_disabled",
            message=(
                "ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY is not configured via settings, Docker secret, "
                "or local secret file, and automatic key generation is disabled. "
                "Provide a stable ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY or set "
                "MOONMIND_ALLOW_LOCAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY_GENERATION=1 for local/dev use."
            ),
            local_secret_path=str(local_secret_path),
        )
        raise ValueError("Could not resolve ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY and automatic generation is disabled.")

    try:
        local_secret_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        new_key = Fernet.generate_key().decode("utf-8")

        # Write securely
        if not local_secret_path.exists():
            local_secret_path.touch(mode=0o600, exist_ok=False)
        local_secret_path.write_text(new_key, encoding="utf-8")
        os.chmod(local_secret_path, stat.S_IRUSR | stat.S_IWUSR)
```

`SecretsService.list_metadata` is annotated to return `Sequence[ManagedSecret]`, but it actually returns `result.all()` from a column-select, i.e. `Sequence[Row]` (id/slug/status/etc). This mismatch makes it easy for callers to accidentally treat results as ORM objects. Either return ORM objects via `scalars().all()` (and ensure ciphertext is deferred/never accessed) or change the return type (and docstring) to reflect the row/DTO shape.

This PR introduces new persisted DB entities (`SecretStatus`, `ManagedSecret`) but there’s no corresponding migration/schema management change in the PR. If the project relies on migrations, production deployments will fail at runtime when the table/enum don’t exist. Add the necessary migration (or document/update the schema creation path) alongside the model addition.
`SecuritySettings` is imported but unused in this test module. If Ruff is run on tests, this will fail unused-import checks; remove it (and any other unused imports) or use it as intended.

The Secrets API endpoints are not protected by authentication/authorization (no `Depends(get_current_user())`), unlike most other `api_service` routers. As-is, anyone who can reach the service can list secret metadata and rotate/delete secrets. Add the same auth dependency used elsewhere (e.g. `_user: User = Depends(get_current_user())`) to every secrets-management endpoint (and consider scoping to admin users if applicable).

The proxy token payload is decrypted but never validated for expiry/TTL or run scoping (it’s effectively a bearer token valid until the encryption key changes). Phase 5 calls for an ephemeral, tightly scoped token. Include `iat`/`exp` (or a short TTL) in the encrypted payload and enforce it in `_verify_and_decode_proxy_token` (and consider binding to workflow/run id and/or audience).

The boot payload JSON is inserted with `|safe` inside a `<script type="application/json">` block. If `generate_boot_payload` ever includes user-controlled `initialData`, this becomes an XSS footgun (e.g. `</script>` injection). Prefer `{{ boot_payload | tojson }}` (or otherwise escape `<`/`</script>` sequences) and parse it client-side.
```suggestion
      {{ boot_payload | tojson }}
```

`InvalidToken` is imported but never used in this module, which will fail Ruff/flake8 unused-import checks in many configurations. Remove the import or use it in a targeted exception handler if you intended to catch that specific decryption error.

This hint says the slug is used by the `'db_encrypted:' resolver`, but the new secret-ref format is `://` (e.g. `db://my-slug`). Update the UI copy to match the actual resolver syntax so operators don’t enter outdated references. ```suggestion
The unique slug used in secret references like db://<slug>.
```

## Pull request overview Implements the Secrets System “Phase 5/6” work by adding managed-secret storage + resolution, introducing proxy-first execution (workers use a synthetic proxy token + internal `/api/v1/proxy/...`), and hardening runtime diagnostics to redact sensitive values. **Changes:** - Added Secrets CRUD API/service + DB model and metadata-only schemas, plus UI surfaces (Secrets Manager page + first-run dashboard alerts). - Introduced proxy-first token injection in `ManagedAgentAdapter` and a new proxy router intended to resolve upstream provider secrets server-side. - Added a new multi-backend SecretRef resolver framework (`env://`, `db://`, `exec://`, `vault://`) and expanded redaction in runtime launcher error paths. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 42 out of 43 changed files in this pull request and generated 26 comments.
